questions.q: Kanada is best remembered for proposing an early version of which scientific concept?
questions.explanation: Kanada proposed that all matter is composed of indivisible particles, an early form of atomic theory.

questions.q: Which school of Indian philosophy did Kanada found?
questions.explanation: Kanada is traditionally credited as the founder of the Vaisheshika school of philosophy.

questions.q: What term did Kanada use for the indivisible particles that make up all matter?
questions.explanation: Kanada called the indivisible particles 'anu', often translated as atom.

questions.q: Kanada is described as 'The Atom Philosopher' because he argued that matter is fundamentally:
options: An illusion with no real substance
questions.explanation: He held that matter cannot be divided forever and is ultimately made of indivisible atoms (anu).

questions.q: The foundational text attributed to Kanada is the:
questions.explanation: Kanada is credited with composing the Vaisheshika Sutra, the core text of the Vaisheshika system.

questions.q: Kanada is regarded as a figure from which civilization's intellectual tradition?
questions.explanation: Kanada was an ancient Indian sage and philosopher.

questions.q: According to Kanada, larger physical objects are formed when atoms:
questions.explanation: Kanada taught that atoms combine to form larger aggregates and ultimately the objects we perceive.

questions.q: Vaisheshika, the school Kanada founded, is best classified as a system of:
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika is one of the classical schools (darshanas) of Indian philosophy.

questions.q: Kanada's central claim about dividing matter was that the process of division:
questions.explanation: He argued that dividing matter cannot go on endlessly and must end at the indivisible anu.

questions.q: Which of these is another name commonly associated with the sage Kanada?
questions.explanation: Kanada is also known by names such as Kashyapa (and Uluka) in the tradition.

questions.q: The Vaisheshika system is traditionally paired and later integrated with which other school of Indian philosophy?
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika became closely allied with the Nyaya school, eventually forming the combined Nyaya-Vaisheshika tradition.

questions.q: In Kanada's Vaisheshika, the world is analyzed in terms of categories called:
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika organizes reality into categories known as padarthas, such as substance, quality and action.

questions.q: How does Kanada's atomism compare in time to the atomism of Greek thinkers like Democritus?
options: It came many centuries after Newton
It belongs to a comparably ancient period of antiquity
It was developed only in the 1800s
It dates to the modern era
questions.explanation: Kanada's atomic ideas belong to ancient antiquity, comparable in era to early Greek atomists.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the substances (dravya) recognized in Kanada's Vaisheshika system?
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika lists substances including earth (prithvi), water, fire, air, ether, time, space, self and mind.

questions.q: In Vaisheshika thought, the smallest stable combination of atoms is often described as a:
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika describes atoms first joining into dyads (and triads), which build up larger bodies.

questions.q: Kanada held that the individual atom itself is:
questions.explanation: Kanada regarded the atom (anu) as eternal, indestructible and the ultimate unit of matter.

questions.q: Which property does Vaisheshika treat as one of the fundamental categories alongside substance?
questions.explanation: Quality (guna) is a core padartha in Vaisheshika, distinct from substance (dravya) and action (karma).

questions.q: Kanada's atomic theory is significant in the history of science mainly because it was:
options: An early philosophical model of matter being made of discrete units
A description of the periodic table
A method for splitting the nucleus
questions.explanation: Its significance lies in being an early conceptual model that matter is built from discrete, indivisible units.

questions.q: The word 'Vaisheshika' is connected to which key concept emphasized by the school?
questions.explanation: The name derives from 'vishesha', the category of particularity that distinguishes individual things.

questions.q: Kanada is traditionally counted among the founders of the six orthodox (astika) schools of Indian philosophy. Vaisheshika is one of these because it:
options: Accepted the authority of the Vedas
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika is an astika (orthodox) school because it accepts the authority of the Vedas.

questions.q: According to the Vaisheshika view, the qualities of a compound object arise from:
options: The atoms and their arrangements that compose it
questions.explanation: The properties of composite objects are explained by the constituent atoms and how they are arranged.

questions.q: Which statement best reflects Kanada's reasoning for why atoms must exist?
options: Because matter glows in the dark
Because endless division is impossible, there must be a smallest indivisible part
questions.explanation: Kanada argued that division cannot proceed infinitely, so there must be a final, indivisible particle.

questions.q: In the Vaisheshika atomic model, three dyads are said to combine to form the smallest perceptible unit, traditionally called a:
questions.explanation: The trasarenu, a triad of dyads, is described as the smallest visible mote of matter in Vaisheshika.

questions.q: Kanada's Vaisheshika enumerates a number of fundamental categories (padarthas). The earliest classical formulation listed how many primary padarthas?
questions.explanation: The original Vaisheshika system listed six padarthas: substance, quality, action, generality, particularity and inherence.

questions.q: Which padartha in Vaisheshika expresses the relation by which a quality permanently belongs to a substance?
questions.explanation: Samavaya, or inherence, is the inseparable relation linking qualities and parts to their substances.

questions.q: A major later commentator who systematized Vaisheshika in the Padartha-dharma-sangraha was:
questions.explanation: Prashastapada wrote the influential Padartha-dharma-sangraha, a key commentary that developed Vaisheshika.

questions.q: In Kanada's scheme, earth, water, fire and air are made of atoms. Which substance is instead treated as all-pervading and non-atomic?
questions.explanation: Ether (akasha), along with time and space, is treated as ubiquitous and non-atomic, unlike the atomic substances earth, water, fire and air.

questions.q: Vaisheshika holds that perceptible objects are non-eternal, yet their constituent atoms are eternal. This combination reflects the school's view that the universe undergoes:
options: Cycles of combination and dissolution of atoms
Continuous infinite division with no atoms
questions.explanation: Vaisheshika describes a cosmos where eternal atoms repeatedly combine and dissolve, while composites come and go.

questions.q: The Vaisheshika and Nyaya schools merged largely because they shared which broad orientation?
options: A realist, analytical approach to knowledge and the categories of reality
A purely ritualistic focus on sacrifice
questions.explanation: Both were realist and analytical, so Nyaya's logic merged naturally with Vaisheshika's categories of reality.

questions.q: Which statement most accurately characterizes the historical significance of Kanada's atomism within the history of science?
options: It was an early speculative, philosophical atomism rather than a quantitative empirical theory
It directly led to the modern Standard Model
It denied that matter could be divided at all
questions.explanation: Kanada's atomism was a pioneering philosophical model of discrete matter, not a quantitative experimental theory.
